TextENGNew: The day of the Watanagashi Festival.
What had I been doing all day?
Nothing. I had no memory of anything.
changeReason: Old 'Nothing in particular' (casual) vs new 'Nothing. I had no memory of anything'; JPN 何の記憶もない = had no memory at all. Fundamentally different meaning.

TextENGNew: I'd simply been idle.@k...Wasteful, meaningless, just staring into space.
changeReason: Old 'I just wanted to play hooky' is inaccurate; JPN 怠惰に無駄に無意味にぼーっと = idle, wasteful, meaningless, dazed. New correctly conveys listless despair.

TextENGNew: I hadn't gotten up until past noon.
I ate whatever instant lunch I could throw together while staring vacantly at some uninteresting television program.
Then, feeling a mild headache coming on, I lay down on the sofa.
If I closed my eyes now, I thought, I probably wouldn't wake again while the sun was still up.
changeReason: Old implies permanent death ('wouldn't wake up again'); new correctly says 'wouldn't wake while the sun was still up' per JPN 日が出ている内には起きれない.

TextENGNew: ...That was fine.
changeReason: Old 'Whatever' (dismissive) vs new 'That was fine' (resigned acceptance); JPN それでもいいか = is that okay too. Different emotional register.

TextENGNew: I surrendered to lethargy.
changeReason: Old 'I decided to sleep' (neutral) vs new 'I surrendered to lethargy'; JPN 惰眠を決め込む means deliberately indulging in lazy sleep.

TextENGNew: ...I'd already told Sis about Satoshi-kun's request to look after Satoko.
There was nothing I personally needed to do today.
I drifted into drowsiness, accompanied by my dull headache...
For me, this day held no more significance than that.
The aunt still believed that the villagers looked down on her because her brother-in-law and his wife had supported the dam project.
There was no denying that she'd suffered some collateral damage during the dam war.
But it wasn't nearly as bad as the aunt convinced herself it still was.
She was paranoid, believing that the villagers were directing malice at her.
And so she turned that perceived malice inside out, harboring a bitter grudge against them in return.
Naturally, this attitude made the other villagers genuinely uncomfortable.@k...And so the neighborhood relations became exactly what the aunt had always feared.
Had the soured relations with the neighbors come first?
Or had the discovery of her husband's affair?
...By now, even the people involved couldn't remember which had started it.
The uncle claimed he'd found another woman because his wife's relentlessly unpleasant attitude had exhausted his love.
The aunt claimed her social standing had crumbled because of her husband's affair, which then ruined her relationships with the neighbors.
Only Satoshi, who occupied the most objective position of all, knew that both had happened more or less simultaneously.
Marital fights became a daily occurrence.
When the husband came home late, the wife would lie in wait, and the two would tear into each other right at the doorstep.
Satoshi had once wondered why, given how much they despised each other, they never considered divorce.
But he alone knew the reason.
It was because of the inheritance his late parents had left behind.
The aunt had hidden the bankbook and the personal seal, never letting the uncle lay a finger on them.
(So the uncle would ransack the house looking for the bankbook and her hidden savings, and pilfer small change from her purse.@kWhich, of course, only fueled more fights.)
...Satoshi couldn't recall his parents ever mentioning that the family was well-off.
But after their deaths, someone involved had told him.
That his late parents had left a substantial sum of money in a bank account.
Satoshi couldn't understand at first how his family, which had always seemed poor, could have had such money.
Most likely,@k...his parents had been asked by the government to provide some form of cooperation regarding the dam project, and had complied.
Winning over other villagers, providing information -- things like that. Details he couldn't be sure of.
They'd probably received undisclosable sums as compensation for that cooperation.
Until that account came to light, the uncle and aunt had shown no inclination whatsoever to take the siblings in.
Only after someone from the financial institution explained the situation did they graciously agree to adopt them.
The ugly, grating fights repeated day after day.
Little by little, the uncle began spending more time at his Okinomiya mistress's place than at home.
Eventually,@kthe husband realized there was no reason to keep coming home just to fight.
When the uncle first stopped coming home, Satoshi felt secretly relieved.
Neither the uncle nor the aunt had ever thought well of the siblings.@kAnd it wasn't uncommon for them to take out their frustrations on Satoshi and Satoko when things went sour between them.
If the friction between the uncle and aunt was the source of their problems, then the two of them living apart shouldn't be a bad thing.
That was what he'd thought at first.
But the wife, even with her husband gone, did not calm down.
Her husband had fled to another woman.
The whole village knew by now, and the villagers were snickering about it behind her back.
It was mortifyingly humiliating.
And then.@kHaving lost her husband as a sparring partner, the aunt directed her pent-up fury squarely at the siblings.
Satoshi had been watching his aunt carefully for a long time.
He'd developed a reasonable understanding of what set her off, what warning signs she showed, and how best to ride out each storm.
He'd learned not to antagonize her needlessly, to react sensitively to the signals of her bad moods, and to dodge and hide his way through the worst of it.
...But Satoko couldn't do any of that.
Satoko openly despised the aunt and made no effort to conceal it.
Satoshi coached her repeatedly, and her attitude did tone down somewhat, but the hatred in her eyes was something she simply couldn't control.
Satoshi did everything in his power to prevent friction between the aunt and Satoko.@kHe smoothed things over.@kHe made excuses.
But for Satoshi -- who, let's not forget, was still just a boy -- the burden this imposed was beyond measure.
He endured with dogged patience, sometimes shielding, sometimes concealing, sometimes maneuvering, trying to maintain at least the appearance of peace.
But... such things were beyond what any individual's effort could sustain.
...The friction between the aunt and Satoko had already passed the point of no return.
A pile of oversized trash caught his eye.
...Whether this was an official disposal site was questionable.
...If it were, the sanitation workers would have hauled it away.
He passed this spot often, so whenever something new appeared in the pile, he noticed immediately.
What caught his attention was a perfectly ordinary office desk, the kind you'd find in any school faculty room.
Someone must have dumped it within the past two or three days.
The aunt never missed the chance to scavenge usable furniture from this trash heap.
Just the other day, she'd found a chest of suitable size and made him help carry it home.
...If he mentioned this newly dumped desk, she'd certainly want to go collect it.
The aunt hated finding bugs or garbage inside the drawers,@kso she always insisted on opening them before moving anything.
He tried opening them.@k...All empty.
He closed the drawers, turned around, and walked five or six steps.
There he stood in a thick patch of underbrush that reached up to his knees.
He laid down the thing he'd been carrying close to his body until today, nestling it into the brush.
Unless you specifically knew to look for it, even the person who'd put it there might lose track of where it was.
...Satisfied, he looked around for landmarks, carefully observing and memorizing them so he wouldn't forget the exact spot.
``Heyyy, old man!!@k@vS06/03/150300163. Another year, another festival!@k@vS06/03/150300164. I'm gonna clean you out again, so brace yourself!``
``Gwa ha ha!@k@vS06/00/yataiA15002. The Sonozaki girl is as fired up as ever!@k@vS06/00/yataiA15003. Oh?@k@vS06/00/yataiA15004. Who's this? A new face!``
``Heh heh, this is Ryuugu Rena.@k@vS06/03/150300166. She looks sweet, but she's quite the competitor.``
``Ahahahaha... N-No, I'm really not.@k@vS06/02/150200150. Nice to meet you!``
``Hey!@k@vS06/00/yataiA15006. You too!@k@vS06/00/yataiA15007. I brought all kinds of prizes this year!@k@vS06/00/yataiA15008. Come take a look!``
``...Rena has a weakness for cute things.``
``Oh yeah, those stuffed animals are right up Rena's alley...``
``Wh-wh-what?!``
``Hauuu!!@k@vS06/02/150200153. So cuuute!@k@vS06/02/150200154. I wanna take it home!!``
``H-Hey, we're not even open yet!! Waah!!!``
``Ah ha ha ha!!@k@vS06/03/150300169. Rena, stop, stop.@k@vS06/03/150300170. He says they're still setting up.@k@vS06/03/150300171. You can go nuts once they're officially open, okay?``
``Hauuu, it's already mine!@k@vS06/02/150200156. I'm taking it hooome!``
Rena's exuberant frenzy drew laughter from everyone present.
But even amid all that liveliness, Satoko didn't crack a smile.
She stood slightly apart from the group, alone and isolated.
...Satoko's eyes remained clouded.
``...Wh-What is it, Rika?``
Perhaps worried about her friend,@kRika sidled up to Satoko with the biggest smile she could muster.
``...You don't have to think about anything today.@k@vS06/05/150500011. Let's just have lots of fun.``
Satoko lowered her head again and turned her eyes away from Rika.
changeReason: Old omits 'turned her eyes away from Rika'; JPN adds 再びうな垂れると、梨花から目をそらす = lowered head again and averted eyes from Rika.

TextENGNew: Even if she enjoyed just this one day,@k...it wouldn't free her from the painful reality awaiting her at home.
``...Satoko.@k@vS06/05/150500013. ...Your mean aunt isn't coming to the festival today.``
changeReason: Old 'your evil aunt isn't around' (generally absent) vs new 'not coming to the festival today'; JPN specifies 今日はお祭りには来ません (won't come to festival today).

TextENGNew: ``I... I know that.``
``...Then laugh lots and have lots of fun.@k@vS06/05/150500015. ...Nipah~~``
``I... appreciate the effort, but... I'm not really in the mood for that.@k@vS06/04/150400016. ...I'm sorry, but... please just leave me be.``
``...Why... won't you smile, Satoko?``
For an instant, Satoko shot back an irritated look.
...A look that said: you already know why, so don't make me spell it out.@kThat kind of look.
Even if she smiled just for today,@kthe reality of living with that vindictive aunt wouldn't change tomorrow.
No matter how much fun the festival might be.
...The moment she went home, the scolding would resume.@kNo matter what she did or didn't do.
...The aunt would find some absurdly trivial fault and scream at her, loud enough for the neighbors to hear, until her voice gave out.
Knowing that reality awaited her, how could anyone tell her to just smile for now...?
She didn't say any of that out loud.
But Satoko's eyes, true to the proverb, spoke as eloquently as any mouth, conveying her feelings to Rika with perfect clarity.
``...Then, Satoko.@k@vS06/05/150500020. ...If your suffering were to end today... would you smile for me?``
Satoko's breath caught for an instant.
She'd thought Rika might be offering some kind of real help.
...But she quickly realized it was nothing more than Rika's way of trying to comfort her.
My suffering, end today?@kBecause of Rika?@kHow?
A moment's thought was enough to know that Rika couldn't possibly do anything about her circumstances.
So the hopeful look that briefly crossed Satoko's face lasted only an instant before being swallowed by resignation.
``Rika.@k@vS06/04/150400018. ...I don't want to hear empty reassurances...``
``...Satoko.``
Rika spoke in a tone of voice Satoko had never heard from her before. Firm. Definitive.
``...It has already been decided.``
After supper, Satoshi slipped out through the window.
All he could hear were the chirping of insects and the faint sounds of the Watanagashi Festival, carried on the breeze.
changeReason: Old omits 'chirping of insects'; JPN 虫の声 (insect chirping) is a detail that sets the atmosphere, absent from old translation.

TextENGNew: The festival was supposed to run until about nine o'clock.
After the communal cotton-tearing ritual, there'd be a raffle drawing, he'd heard.
In other words, the people who'd planned to head home for dinner were already back.@kAnd those who'd resolved to stay for the final raffle were still at the shrine. The village had divided cleanly.
changeReason: Old describes a typology ('two types of people'); JPN describes the current state of separation at this hour — a temporal description, not a categorization.

TextENGNew: Either relaxing at home, or attending the festival at the shrine.
One or the other, nothing in between.
And on top of that, the area around Satoshi's house was particularly isolated.@kThere weren't many neighbors.
changeReason: New adds 'the area was particularly isolated' per JPN とても寂しい, a detail missing from old that explains why no one witnessed anything.

TextENGNew: So naturally, there wasn't a soul to be seen on the streets.
...In already-lonely Hinamizawa, there could be no lonelier night than this one.
Satoshi had known, intellectually, that it would be like this.
But he couldn't rest easy until he confirmed it with his own eyes.
He was barefoot, but he took off running without a second thought.
...Running barefoot, he felt as though he'd become some kind of wild animal.
He felt like he could run faster, more nimbly, more powerfully than ever before.
Not just his legs.
His vision.
His hearing.
His sense of smell.
...No, he felt as though he'd gained something beyond the five senses -- a sixth sense, almost supernatural.
changeReason: New adds 'supernatural' (JPN 超常的) to describe the sixth sense; old just says senses were 'keener.' The supernatural qualifier is significant.

TextENGNew: If he crouched low like this, pressing himself to the ground, he felt he could read the presence of every person for miles around.@k...Or so it seemed.
changeReason: New adds 'for miles around' (JPN 四里四方 = four ri in every direction), specifying the extraordinary range absent from old.

TextENGNew: It was truly strange.@k...Today felt like another world entirely.
Up until the very last moment before today arrived, he'd been so tense, so agitated, locked in a desperate internal struggle.
But now he felt as though he'd shed his old skin... as though the person he was now was a completely different being.
``All riiiight!@k@vS06/00/yataiB15002. I'm going to explain the raffle rules now!@k@vS06/00/yataiB15003. Everyone, please gather around the tower!``
changeReason: Old 'gather around the altar' vs new 'gather around the tower'; JPN やぐら = stage/scaffold/tower, not altar. Different physical object.

TextENGNew: The Watanagashi Festival was about to reach its climax: the raffle drawing.
The women's association members who'd been doing the bon odori dance, along with the crowd that had stuck around for the raffle, all converged on the tower in a surge of excitement.
changeReason: New specifies 'women's association members' (JPN 婦人会) and 'bon odori dance' (JPN 盆踊り), cultural details absent from old.

TextENGNew: This raffle was not a longstanding tradition.
It was a brand-new event, proposed by a committee member as this year's highlight and approved for implementation.
changeReason: Old 'decided to hold one from this year onward' (committee decision) vs new 'brand-new event proposed by a committee member'; JPN clarifies it was one person's proposal.

TextENGNew: The prizes were supposed to be donated by the committee members out of goodwill, but when Sonozaki Oryou saw what had been collected, she declared them 'pathetic' and had them all thrown out.
changeReason: New adds that it was specifically 'Sonozaki Oryou' who saw and rejected the prizes, a character identification absent from old.

TextENGNew: Oryou decreed that prizes should be at minimum of a certain caliber, and purchased a new television, a washing machine, a fan, and other such items.
changeReason: Old 'took the initiative and bought' vs new 'decreed that prizes should be at minimum of a certain caliber'; JPN conveys Oryou's imperious standard-setting.

TextENGNew: The committee's modest, bring-what-you-can arrangement was overridden by Oryou's single decree, transforming the prizes into a lavish spread.@kAll purchased with her personal funds, no less.
changeReason: New adds 'All purchased with her personal funds' (JPN 全てお魎の私費), a significant detail about Oryou's generosity missing from old.

TextENGNew: Despite being the one who'd kicked out all the original prizes, Oryou was apparently furious that 'the committee members lacked backbone.' A difficult woman indeed.
changeReason: New adds the irony that Oryou herself kicked out the original prizes yet blamed others; old omits this contradiction present in JPN.

TextENGNew: Some committee members privately felt that organizing this raffle again next year would be extremely difficult, and suspected this first time would also be the last.
``Thanks to you, Oryou-san, we've got such magnificent prizes.@k@vS06/18/153300002. That must have cost quite a bit, no?@k@vS06/18/153300003. I can reimburse you from the operating fund.``
``Fool! Don't insult me with reimbursement over that pittance.@k@vS06/17/152300002. It was never about the money.@k@vS06/17/152300003. ...Good grief.``
``Now, now, don't say that...@k@vS06/18/153300005. This raffle only happened because of you, Oryou-san.``
``As if it should fall on me alone to make everything happen.@k@vS06/17/152300005. Ridiculous.``
Village chief Kimiyoshi seemed to be having a difficult time trying to soothe Oryou's temper.
Then one of the committee members came sprinting into the headquarters tent, gasping for breath.
Seeing his alarmed state, Kimiyoshi's first thought was that there'd been an accident -- perhaps a child was hurt.
``What's the matter, Makino-san?@k@vS06/18/153300007. If you keep panting like that, you'll burst your heart.``
changeReason: Old 'I hope you're not having a heart attack' vs new 'you'll burst your heart'; JPN 心臓が破れちゃう = heart will burst. Different joke construction.

TextENGNew: Kimiyoshi deliberately made light of it to try to calm him down.
But Makino, his expression frozen, leaned in and whispered something urgently into Kimiyoshi's ear.
Even without hearing the words, the exchange between Kimiyoshi and Makino made it blatantly obvious that bad news had arrived.
``...Oryou-san.@k@vS06/18/153300009. I have some unfortunate news, if I may?``
Kimiyoshi returned to Oryou's side with Makino and dropped his voice to a near-whisper.
With a grave expression, he murmured to her in a low voice, repeating word-for-word exactly what Makino had told him.
``Nothing to fret about.@k@vS06/17/152300007. If something happens thrice, it'll happen a fourth time. That's just how it goes.``
``Ahahahaha...@k@vS06/18/153300013. Oryou-san, that really isn't funny...``
``Wasn't meant to be.@k@vS06/17/152300009. ...Just another headache to deal with.``
``The police are currently trying to identify the body.@k@vS06/18/153300015. Apparently the face was badly damaged, making identification difficult...``
``...Even without a face, one can hazard a fair guess...``
changeReason: New adds 'Even without a face' (JPN 顔が分からんでも), contextualizing how Oryou can still guess the identity. Old omits this detail.

TextENGNew: ``Huh?@k@vS06/18/153300017. ...Do you have an idea who it might be?``
Oryou fixed Kimiyoshi with an irritated glare, then broke into a cackling laugh.

``Ha ha ha ha ha, you fool!@k@vS06/17/152300012. Obviously it's some faithless wretch who couldn't be bothered to show their face at the Watanagashi Festival!@k@vS06/17/152300013. If they'd paid proper respects to Oyashiro-sama, they'd have nothing to fear!``
Oryou cackled on, while Kimiyoshi and Makino seemed uncertain whether it was appropriate to laugh along...
``...Hmm.@k@vS06/19/153100002. ...This is a brutal one.``
``Kuma-chan.@k@vS06/11/150700002. Every death is brutal, unless the deceased wished for it.@k@vS06/11/150700003. Let's pay our respects.@k@vS06/11/150700004. And do our jobs.``
changeReason: Old includes multiple lines of investigation dialogue that new condenses; the new version omits Ooishi's procedural questions about pocket contents and the scene.

TextENGNew: The investigators wearing 'Forensics' armbands shook their heads in response.

``Wearing sandals, dressed casually.@k@vS06/19/153100004. Probably a local resident.@k@vS06/19/153100005. If we canvass the area with a description of the clothing, we should get an ID pretty quickly.``
``...That's the play.@k@vS06/11/150700007. The section chief's already hinting this case might get classified as confidential.@k@vS06/11/150700008. Keep that in mind, will you?``
``Roger.``
Kumagai called over several officers and appeared to be giving them detailed instructions.
``Dr. Irie has arrived.@k@vS06/19/153100008. Doctor, it's over here.``
A clinic vehicle pulled up, and a young physician could be seen approaching.
changeReason: Old 'A young doctor got out of his car' vs new 'A clinic vehicle pulled up'; JPN specifies 診療所の車 = clinic vehicle, identifying it as an official vehicle.

TextENGNew: ``Pardon me...@k@vS06/10/151000101. Sorry for the delay.@k@vS06/10/151000102. I was helping with the raffle when I got the call.``
``Doctor, you must've had a few at the festival tonight.@k@vS06/11/150700010. You sure the alcohol's worn off?@k@vS06/11/150700011. Maybe I should have you blow up a balloon for me, nah ha ha.``
changeReason: Old 'run a breathalyzer test' vs new 'have you blow up a balloon'; JPN 風船でも膨らましてもらっちゃおうかな preserves the humorous euphemism about balloon-blowing.

TextENGNew: ``Rest assured.@k@vS06/10/151000104. I had my assistant do the driving.``
``Very thorough of you.@k@vS06/11/150700013. ...Hey, let the doctor through.``
An area had been heavily cordoned off with blue tarps.
An officer lifted the edge of the tarp and ushered Ooishi and Irie inside.
Forensics personnel were photographing the scene from every conceivable angle.
Scattered blood spatters, a displaced sandal -- everything remained exactly as it had been found,@kmarked with chalk outlines and small lettered placards.
``...This isn't something I'm accustomed to seeing.``
Irie pulled out a handkerchief and pressed it firmly over his nose.
``When the smell gets to you, switch to breathing through your mouth.@k@vS06/11/150700015. ...Though with something this pungent, your eyes might 'smell' it too.@k@vS06/11/150700016. Nah ha ha...``
It was... a middle-aged woman? Lying face-up, drenched in blood.
The hair, the clothing, the general build -- it was almost certainly a middle-aged woman.
Why the hesitation in saying so with certainty?@k...Because the face had been completely obliterated.
``...This is horrific.``

``Nine times out of ten, this kind of thing is personal. A grudge.@k@vS06/11/150700018. Based on the blood patterns and the sandal treads, there's no doubt the killing happened right here.@k@vS06/11/150700019. But as you can see, this is a secluded spot. No one around to witness it.``

``Continuing to bludgeon the head until it caved in completely -- that goes beyond anything rational.@k@vS06/11/150700021. But here's the thing.@k@vS06/11/150700022. Take a look at the arms and hands.``
changeReason: Old 'unusual to see a culprit who would bludgeon' vs new 'goes beyond anything rational'; JPN まともな話じゃない = not a sane/rational thing. Different analytical framing.

TextENGNew: Ooishi snapped on a pair of thin rubber gloves and lifted one of the body's arms.
``...See?@k@vS06/11/150700024. The arms are clean. Unmarked.``
``You're saying there are no signs the victim tried to shield herself or resist?``
``Sharp mind there, doctor.@k@vS06/11/150700026. ...I suspect the victim was knocked unconscious by the very first blow -- concussion or something similar.``
changeReason: Old 'Right you are' vs new 'Sharp mind there, doctor'; JPN さぁすが高学歴 = 'as expected of someone highly educated' — a teasing compliment old misses entirely.

TextENGNew: ``And then the perpetrator kept striking to make certain of the kill.@k@vS06/11/150700028. Furthermore, I believe the victim collapsed face-down initially.@k@vS06/11/150700029. The back of the head took most of the damage first.``
``...The body is face-up now.``

``After thoroughly destroying the back of the skull,@k@vS06/11/150700031. even after clearly feeling the cranium give way, the perpetrator deliberately flipped the body over.@k@vS06/11/150700032. And then proceeded to destroy the face as well.``
changeReason: New adds detail about 'destroying the back of the skull first' from JPN 後頭部を散々潰して, specifying the sequence of attack.

TextENGNew: Irie looked as though he might retch, overwhelmed equally by the suffocating stench and Ooishi's almost enthusiastic narration.

``Attacking the face of a corpse -- in my experience, that's a very strong indicator of a personal grudge.@k@vS06/11/150700034. A deep, intimate one at that.@k@vS06/11/150700035. Which means the perpetrator was someone close to the victim.``
``...I see you didn't call me here for resuscitation.``
``Doctor.@k@vS06/11/150700038. Can you tell who this person is?@k@vS06/11/150700039. ...An identification would save us considerable legwork.``
Irie looked at the body once more.
He quickly averted his eyes, his face taking on a thoughtful expression.
...After a moment.
Without saying a word, Irie stepped out from behind the blue tarp.
``...I'm sorry.@k@vS06/10/151000112. ...I'm afraid I can't tell.``
``...Really?``
Ooishi peered into Irie's eyes with a slow, knowing grin.
As if to say: I know you know, and you're hiding it.
changeReason: New explicitly states Ooishi's suspicion ('I know you know') per JPN's implication; old is vaguer about the nature of the look.

TextENGNew: ``...I'm not hiding anything.``

``...Nah ha ha ha.@k@vS06/11/150700043. Must be the alcohol still in your system, doctor.@k@vS06/11/150700044. Once your head clears, it might come back to you.@k@vS06/11/150700045. If it does, give me a call, won't you?``
Ooishi gave Irie a hearty slap on the back.
Irie mumbled something about wanting fresh air and hurried away, avoiding the crowd.
Kumagai came running over as Irie left.
``Ooishi-san, the section chief is on the radio.@k@vS06/19/153100010. Car one.``
``...Dr. Irie was lying through his teeth.``
``Nothing, nothing.@k@vS06/11/150700048. Hurry with the canvass for the victim's identity.@k@vS06/11/150700049. She was almost certainly from the immediate area.``
``Ooishi-san.@k@vS06/19/153100013. He's here!!``
``Thank you for coming, and so late at night.@k@vS06/11/150700051. I know you have school tomorrow. I really am sorry about this.``
``...It's fine.``
``And where is your father?``
``...My stepfather doesn't... come home very often.``
``Oh?@k@vS06/11/150700055. Since when?@k@vS06/11/150700056. When was the last time he came home?``

``...I'm not sure.@k@vS06/07/151200257. From what I've overheard from their fights, he seems to be living with some woman who works at a cabaret in Okinomiya or something.@k@vS06/07/151200258. The last time I saw him... I can't quite remember.``
``...I see, I see.@k@vS06/11/150700058. ...Do you know how to contact your father?``
changeReason: Old 'Do you have your father's address?' vs new 'Do you know how to contact your father?'; JPN 連絡先 = contact information, not address.

TextENGNew: ``You said he's living with a woman from a cabaret.``
changeReason: Old 'a club' vs new 'a cabaret'; JPN キャバレー = cabaret, a specific type of establishment.

TextENGNew: ``Do you know which establishment?``
``...No.@k@vS06/07/151200261. I only know it's somewhere in Okinomiya.``
``What's your father's name?``
``Houjou Teppei.``
Ooishi signaled with his chin, and Kumagai, who'd been jotting everything down in his notebook, nodded in acknowledgment.
``Your mother and father -- they didn't get along well?``
changeReason: Old asks an open question 'Were they getting along well?'; new presupposes the answer 'they didn't get along well?' per JPN's negative framing.

TextENGNew: ``...I can't speak to the full extent of it, but...@k@vS06/07/151200264. ...I don't think they got along.``
``Hmm...@k@vS06/11/150700064. ...I hope you'll forgive me for being blunt, but if you know of anyone who might have held a grudge against your mother, I'd appreciate you telling us.``
``...I don't know.``
``Nah ha ha...@k@vS06/11/150700066. I see.@k@vS06/11/150700067. Well, if anything comes to mind later, please let me know then.@k@vS06/11/150700068. Here's my card.``

``We may need to speak with you again in the coming days.@k@vS06/11/150700071. And if you manage to get in touch with your father, please tell him to contact Detective Ooishi at the Okinomiya Police Department as soon as possible.``
``...Kuma-chan, drive him home, would you?``
``Oh, no. That's all right.@k@vS06/07/151200268. ...I can walk home by myself.``

``Now, now, I insist. Please let us give you a ride.@k@vS06/11/150700075. ...The person who attacked your mother may still be in the area.@k@vS06/11/150700076. It's not safe to be walking alone at this hour.``
``.........Th-Thank you for the concern.@k@vS06/07/151200270. But... really, I'm fine...``
Satoshi tried to decline Kumagai's escort, but Kumagai, having received his orders from Ooishi, wasn't in a position to back down.
Satoshi had no choice but to accept the ride.
He watched Satoshi's retreating back for a moment.
...Then, as if suddenly remembering something he'd forgotten to say, Ooishi called out.
``Houjou-san...!``
``It'll be all right.@k@vS06/11/150700079. Don't you worry.@k@vS06/11/150700080. We'll catch the culprit soon enough.``
``...Do you have... any leads...?``
``...What kind?``
``...That's classified. Part of the investigation.@k@vS06/11/150700084. Nah ha ha ha ha...!``
On the surface, it might have looked like Ooishi was joking around to cheer up a boy devastated by his mother's murder.

``...The thing is,@k@vS06/11/150700086. killing someone gets the emotions running pretty hot.@k@vS06/11/150700087. ...Now, sure, you occasionally get the rare robot who can do it in cold blood, but most of the time, emotions are running wild.``
``When emotions run wild... what kind of evidence does that leave...?``
``When a person's emotions reach their absolute peak, the body produces all sorts of reactions.@k@vS06/11/150700090. ...Excessive sweating, for instance... or hair loss.``

``Finding the perpetrator's hair at a murder scene is actually quite common.@k@vS06/11/150700092. Of course, this is an outdoor scene.@k@vS06/11/150700093. If it were carpeted, a single hair would practically shine like a beacon, but with this terrain, it's a bit trickier.``
``Did you find any hair from the culprit...?``

``Who knows?@k@vS06/11/150700094. The forensics team has been doing sweeps of the entire area for a while now.@k@vS06/11/150700095. Still waiting on results.@k@vS06/11/150700096. But the team is top-notch, so if there's anything to find, they'll find it.``
``.........I have to get up early tomorrow. I'd like to go home now.``
``Of course.@k@vS06/11/150700099. Kuma-chan, if you would.``
``Sure thing.``

``Well then, Houjou-san.@k@vS06/11/150700101. We'll be in touch.@k@vS06/11/150700102. I'll let you know if there are any developments.@k@vS06/11/150700103. ...And if you think of anything, don't hesitate to reach out.``
